evals.prompt: I want to add team billing to our SaaS app. Right now each user has their own subscription but we're getting requests from companies who want to pay for a group of users under one account.
evals.expected_output: A vision & scope interview that elaborates the vague idea into a clear concept, explores existing billing/user models for feasibility, resolves all 6 domains (problem, stakeholders, feasibility, scope, risks, recommendation) one question at a time with recommendations, and produces a scope document with go/no-go verdict. Should ask multiple questions about problem/motivation before moving to other domains. Should explore risks with the user, not fabricate them. Should NOT ask functional requirements questions (specific billing rules, permissions, field-level decisions, approval workflows) — defer to /define. Should NOT include data models, behavior specs, or phased build plans. Should end by suggesting '/define'.

evals.prompt: We need a way for users to share dashboards with people outside our org. Like a public link or something, but we need to control what data is visible.
evals.expected_output: A vision & scope interview that elaborates the concept (what kind of sharing, rough audience), explores existing dashboard/permissions models for feasibility, assesses whether the current auth system can support external sharing, and produces a scope document with feasibility assessment addressing security constraints. Should ask multiple questions about problem/motivation. Should explore risks with the user. Should NOT ask functional requirements questions (link expiration rules, data filtering mechanics, specific permission models) — defer to /define. Should NOT dive into implementation details. Should end by suggesting '/define'.

evals.prompt: I want to add AI-powered search to our docs site. Users keep complaining they can't find things and our current keyword search is pretty bad.
evals.expected_output: A vision & scope interview that elaborates the concept (semantic vs keyword, scope of content), explores existing search infrastructure for feasibility, identifies key risks (vector DB dependency, embedding model costs, search quality validation), and produces a scope document with a clear recommendation. Should ask multiple questions about problem/motivation. Should explore risks with the user, not fabricate them. Should NOT ask functional requirements questions (ranking algorithms, result display rules, indexing behavior) — defer to /define. Should NOT design the embedding pipeline or specify the vector database. Should end by suggesting '/define'.

evals.prompt: I want candidates in our election app to have a profile that voters can see when voting.
evals.expected_output: A vision & scope interview that elaborates the vague idea into a clear concept (what kind of profile content, who manages it at a high level), assesses feasibility by exploring existing candidate/election models, and produces a scope document with a go/no-go recommendation. Questions should stay at the concept/capability level — e.g., 'Is this text-only or do you need photos/media?' NOT 'Should profiles lock during voting?' or 'Per-candidacy or per-user?' Should NOT pre-answer /define questions about specific behaviors, permissions rules, visibility rules, or approval workflows. Should explore risks with the user. Should end by suggesting '/define'.

evals.prompt: I want to build a real-time collaborative spreadsheet editor inside our project management app. Like Google Sheets but embedded. Users keep asking for it.
evals.expected_output: A vision & scope interview that explores the idea thoroughly but reaches a no-go or investigate-further verdict due to feasibility concerns (CRDT/OT complexity, significant infrastructure requirements, buy-vs-build economics favoring integration over build). Should push hard on evidence: how many users asked? What do they actually do today? What would they stop using? Should explore whether an integration (embed existing tool) solves the problem. Should NOT rubber-stamp a go verdict on an obviously high-risk, high-cost feature. Verdict should follow logically from findings. Should end with actionable next step even on no-go. All findings must be expressed in plain language — no technical terms like CRDT, OT, or WebSocket.

evals.prompt: We should do something with AI. Our competitors all have AI features and we're falling behind.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT accept the vague framing at face value. Pushes relentlessly on problem & motivation: What specific competitor features? What user problems would AI solve? What evidence of 'falling behind' (churn data, user complaints, lost deals)? Should not let the user stay at 'we need AI' — must drill down to a concrete capability before feasibility assessment is meaningful. Should probe whether the real problem is competitive positioning vs actual user need. If user cannot articulate a specific problem AI would solve after thorough probing, should reach a no-go or investigate-further verdict rather than scoping a vague 'add AI' feature. Should NOT propose specific AI implementations (that's architect territory). Should end by suggesting '/define' only if a concrete scope emerges.
